flashcards.front: What is the mathematical expectation of a random variable?
flashcards.back: The mathematical expectation of a random variable is a measure of the center of the distribution, representing the long-run average value that the variable would take on if the experiment were repeated many times.
The mathematical expectation of a random variable X is typically denoted by E(X) or μ (mu).

flashcards.front: Formula for mathematical expectation of a discrete random variable
flashcards.back: E(X) = ΣxP(X=x), where x represents the possible values of the random variable X and P(X=x) represents the probability of each value.

flashcards.front: Formula for mathematical expectation of a continuous random variable
flashcards.back: E(X) = ∫xf(x)dx, where f(x) represents the probability density function of the random variable X.

flashcards.front: What is the mathematical expectation of a constant?
flashcards.back: The mathematical expectation of a constant is the constant itself, since a constant can be thought of as a random variable that always takes on the same value.
